title: Writing the research claim without overclaiming
learningObjectives: Distinguish between rigorous scientific claims and unsupported overclaims.
Apply a checklist to validate the strength and boundaries of a research claim.
Synthesize experimental results (metric, baseline, p-value, effect size) into a precise statement.

whyThisLesson.text:
The fastest way to get a paper rejected at ICLR or NeurIPS is to make claims that your experiments do not support. Mechanistic interpretability is particularly vulnerable to 'illusion of understanding'—assuming a feature means exactly what we want it to mean.

In this final lesson, we learn the discipline of the scientific claim. We will learn how to state exactly what the data shows, acknowledge alternative explanations, and present SetConCA honestly.

sections.bridgeFrom: We have the hypotheses and the data. Now we must translate the data into human knowledge, bound by the limits of our experimental design.

intuition.analogyLimit: A map represents physical space; our claims represent high-dimensional statistical probabilities.
intuition.text: A good researcher is like a surveyor. You do not say 'I have discovered the entire continent' when you have only mapped one coastline. You say, 'I have mapped 50 miles of coastline, and within this region, the terrain is rocky.' Overclaiming is drawing dragons on the unmapped parts and calling it a geography paper.

toyExample.description: Drafting the core result of the toy experiment.

steps.action: Bad claim: 'SetConCA discovers the model's true concepts.'
steps.result: Unfalsifiable, vague, ignores baselines and metrics.

steps.action: Better claim: 'SetConCA finds purer concepts than standard SAEs.'
steps.result: Still lacks specific metrics, controls, and effect sizes.

steps.action: Good claim: 'At matched FVU and sparsity, SetConCA features have 15% higher concept purity than TopK SAE features on our toy benchmark (3 seeds, p < 0.01, d=0.8).'
steps.result: Precise, bounded, verifiable, and statistically sound.
steps.insight: A strong claim defines exactly under what conditions it holds true.

visual.question: Is your claim ready for publication?
visual.description: A flowchart guiding a claim through the 7-point checklist: Metric, Baseline, Seeds, Alternative Explanations, Failure Modes, Negative Controls, Downstream Transfer.

technical.text: Every hypothesis from Lesson 4 (H1-H5) requires a master table entry before it becomes a claim. For each hypothesis, we must fill in: the metric used, the value for SetConCA, the value for the baseline, the p-value, the effect size, a plausible alternative explanation, and the remaining weakness of the evidence.
definitions: Overclaiming: Stating conclusions that extend beyond the specific evidence provided by the experiments.
Effect size (e.g., Cohen's d): A quantitative measure of the magnitude of a phenomenon, distinct from statistical significance.
Alternative explanation: A different mechanism that could produce the identical experimental results, which must be addressed or ruled out.

mathematics.label: Statistical Significance vs Effect Size

symbols.p\\text{-value}: Probability of observing the data given the null hypothesis

mathematics.plainExplanation: A p-value tells you if the difference is real (not noise). The effect size (d) tells you if the difference actually matters.
mathematics.whyNeeded: With large enough samples (like millions of tokens), trivial differences become statistically significant. Effect size proves importance.
mathematics.ifRemoved: We might claim a 'significant' improvement that is practically useless (e.g., 0.1% higher purity).
mathematics.encourages: Focusing on meaningful advancements rather than purely statistical artifacts.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the effect holds in all domains.

evidence.claim: At matched FVU and sparsity, SetConCA features have 15% higher concept purity than TopK SAE features on our toy benchmark.
evidence.label: [Hypothesis]
evidence.paraphrase: SetConCA yields a measurable, significant improvement in purity under controlled conditions.

failureModes.name: The Illusion of Universality
failureModes.mechanism: Assuming a method that works on a toy model or one specific layer of a 7B model will work everywhere.
failureModes.symptom: Broad claims like 'SetConCA solves polysemanticity in LLMs' that fall apart on different architectures.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Apply the checklist: Have you tested multiple model families and scales?
failureModes.mitigation: Scope the claim strictly to the models and layers tested.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: Interpretability methods are notoriously fragile across models; we must be explicitly bounded.

setconcaConnection.relevance: This is how we present SetConCA to the scientific community.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: Framing the results transparently to survive peer review.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: Populate the master claim table for all SetConCA experiments, explicitly identifying the remaining weaknesses for the discussion section.

exercises.prompt: Critique this claim: 'SetConCA completely eliminates feature absorption, as shown by our experiments.'
exercises.reveal: It is an overclaim. 'Completely eliminates' is absolute and highly unlikely. It doesn't specify the baseline, the metric, or the dataset. A better claim: 'SetConCA reduces the rate of feature absorption by X% compared to TopK SAEs on dataset Y.'

masteryQuestions.prompt: Design a controlled experiment that would provide the strongest possible evidence for ONE of SetConCA's claims. Specify: the hypothesis, the metric, the baseline, the negative control, the expected effect size, and one result that would falsify the claim.
rubric: Identifies a concrete, measurable metric and a valid baseline.
Includes a negative control (e.g., random sets).
Specifies a quantitative expected effect size.
Defines a clear, objective condition that would falsify the hypothesis.
masteryQuestions.badVersion: Test SetConCA on Llama 3 to see if it is better than SAE.
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Hypothesis: SetConCA yields better cross-seed stability than TopK SAEs. Metric: Average Jaccard similarity of active features across 5 random seeds. Baseline: TopK SAE matched at FVU=0.05. Negative Control: SetConCA trained on random sets. Expected Effect Size: d > 1.0 improvement over baseline. Falsification condition: If the negative control (random sets) achieves equal or better cross-seed stability than the semantic sets, the hypothesis that semantic contrastive learning is driving the stability is falsified.

bridgeToNext.text: The curriculum is complete. You now have the tools to understand, evaluate, and contribute to research on sparse autoencoders, multi-view representation learning, and set-based concept analysis. The next step is to implement and test SetConCA on real language model activations.
